original_abstract:
Distributional semantic models represent word meanings by analyzing patterns of co-occurrence within large bodies of text. While these models have become a cornerstone of computational linguistics—providing reliable measures of semantic relatedness for various applications—they rely exclusively on textual data. This creates a "grounding" problem, as text alone is an impoverished source of information compared to the rich, multi-sensory experiences that shape human knowledge.

In this paper, we bridge the gap between abstract text and perceptual reality by incorporating computer vision into distributional models. We utilize techniques that automatically identify discrete "visual words" within images, allowing a word’s computational representation to include its co-occurrence with the visual elements it typically describes. We introduce a flexible architecture designed to fuse text- and image-based data into a single multimodal space. Through a series of empirical evaluations, we demonstrate that our integrated model consistently outperforms traditional, text-only approaches. Furthermore, we show that visual data provides a unique and complementary layer of semantic information that text-based models often fail to capture.
humanized_abstract:
There are various approaches to mod-eling meaning in words using distributional semantic models. The common theme to them all is that word meanings are modelled based on patterns of co-occurrence detected in large texts. Such models are very popular and have been shown to give high quality measurements of different types of semantic relations between words. Their exclusive foundation on text leads to what has been termed the 'grounding' problem. While human understanding is almost always based on multi-sensory experience, consisting of what we hear, see, taste, etc., the textual medium provides a very restricted set of sensory information compared to what is experienced in real life.

This paper combines computer vision with distributional models to link words in texts with their visual counterparts in images. We exploit a paradigm that is analogous to state-of-the-art object recognition methods that classify images into a set of predefined “visual words.” The basic idea is to include as part of a word’s semantic representation of co-occurring visual words that are typically found in images depicting instances of the word. We describe a versatile framework for merging text and image information into a single multimodal semantic space. We evaluate our model with a variety of experiments that confirm that our word embeddings that combine text and image outperform their text-only counterparts. Importantly, we also show that including visual information into semantic space captures a different semantic facet of meaning that is lost when only text information is used.
